title: LFU Cache
description: Implement LFU Cache using DLL + hashmap

examples.input: capacity=2, operations: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2), get(3), put(4,4), get(1), get(3), get(4)
examples.explanation: Initialize cache with capacity 2. put(1,1): array=[(1,1,freq=1,ts=1)]. put(2,2): array=[(1,1,1,1), (2,2,1,2)]. get(1): find key 1, value=1, freq=2, ts=3, sort by freq asc then ts asc: array=[(2,2,1,2), (1,1,2,3)], return 1. put(3,3): cache full (2 items), sort: [(2,2,1,2), (1,1,2,3)], evict (2,2) (LFU), add (3,3,1,4), sort: [(3,3,1,4), (1,1,2,3)]. get(2): key 2 not found, return -1. get(3): find key 3, value=3, freq=2, ts=5, sort: [(1,1,2,3), (3,3,2,5)], return 3. put(4,4): cache full, sort: [(1,1,2,3), (3,3,2,5)], evict (1,1) (same freq, older ts), add (4,4,1,6), sort: [(4,4,1,6), (3,3,2,5)]. get(1): key 1 not found, return -1. get(3): find key 3, value=3, freq=3, ts=7, sort: [(4,4,1,6), (3,3,3,7)], return 3. get(4): find key 4, value=4, freq=2, ts=8, sort: [(3,3,3,7), (4,4,2,8)], return 4.

examples.input: capacity=3, operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), get(1), get(2), put(4,40), get(3), get(4)

approaches.name: Approach 1: Array with Sorting on Every Operation
approaches.summary: Maintain all cache entries in an array, sorting by frequency and recency on each get/put operation. Use linear search to find items. This brute force approach is conceptually simple but extremely inefficient for real-world use.

ladders.title: Full Problem — LFU Cache Implementation with Array Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete LFU cache implementation using array storage with sorting on every operation.
ladders.explanation: This approach implements an LFU (Least Frequently Used) cache by storing all entries in an array and sorting them on every get and put operation. The intuition is simple: maintain all cache entries in a single data structure, and whenever we need to determine which item to evict (when cache is full), we sort the array to find the least frequently used item (with tie-breaking by recency). For get operations, we increment frequency and resort. For put operations, we either update existing items or add new ones, potentially evicting the LFU item when at capacity. The algorithm works because sorting brings the least frequently used (and least recent for ties) item to the front of the array, making eviction straightforward. However, this brute-force method is extremely inefficient with O(n log n) time per operation due to sorting, and O(n) space for storing n items. It connects to real-world scenarios as a conceptual baseline before optimizing with hash maps and linked lists. Common mistakes include forgetting to update timestamps for recency tie-breaking, incorrect sorting logic (frequency first, then timestamp), and not handling cache misses properly. The pseudocode: class LFUCache: __init__(capacity), get(key): linear search for key, if found: increment freq, update timestamp, sort array, return value; else return -1. put(key, value): if key exists: update value, increment freq, update timestamp; else: if at capacity: sort array, remove first item; add new item with freq=1, current timestamp; sort array. Time: O(n log n) per operation, Space: O(n).

examples.input: capacity=2, operations: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2), get(3), put(4,4), get(1), get(3), get(4)
examples.trace: Initialize cache with capacity 2. put(1,1): array=[(1,1,freq=1,ts=1)]. put(2,2): array=[(1,1,1,1), (2,2,1,2)]. get(1): find key 1, value=1, freq=2, ts=3, sort by freq asc then ts asc: array=[(2,2,1,2), (1,1,2,3)], return 1. put(3,3): cache full (2 items), sort: [(2,2,1,2), (1,1,2,3)], evict (2,2) (LFU), add (3,3,1,4), sort: [(3,3,1,4), (1,1,2,3)]. get(2): key 2 not found, return -1. get(3): find key 3, value=3, freq=2, ts=5, sort: [(1,1,2,3), (3,3,2,5)], return 3. put(4,4): cache full, sort: [(1,1,2,3), (3,3,2,5)], evict (1,1) (same freq, older ts), add (4,4,1,6), sort: [(4,4,1,6), (3,3,2,5)]. get(1): key 1 not found, return -1. get(3): find key 3, value=3, freq=3, ts=7, sort: [(4,4,1,6), (3,3,3,7)], return 3. get(4): find key 4, value=4, freq=2, ts=8, sort: [(3,3,3,7), (4,4,2,8)], return 4.

examples.input: capacity=3, operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), get(1), get(2), put(4,40), get(3), get(4)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting Cache Entries by Frequency and Recency
ladders.desc: Implement the sorting logic to order cache entries for LFU eviction decisions.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the LFU cache implementation using array sorting. The intuition is that to determine which item to evict when the cache is full, we need to identify the least frequently used item, and if multiple items have the same frequency, we evict the least recently used among them. By sorting the array of cache entries, we can bring the LFU item (with tie-breaking by recency) to the front, making eviction a simple removal of the first element. The algorithm works by defining a comparison function that sorts entries first by frequency in ascending order (lower frequency means less used), then by timestamp in ascending order (older timestamp means less recent). Each cache entry is a tuple (key, value, frequency, timestamp). The step-by-step process: 1. Maintain an array of entries. 2. On each get or put operation that modifies frequencies or adds/removes entries, call the sort function. 3. The sort function uses a custom comparator: compare two entries a and b; if a.frequency != b.frequency, return a.frequency - b.frequency; else return a.timestamp - b.timestamp. This ensures that after sorting, the first entry is the one with the smallest frequency and, if tied, the smallest timestamp. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting n entries, and space is O(1) extra for the sort (in-place). This feeds into the full problem by providing the mechanism to always know which item to evict and to maintain order after updates. Common mistakes include sorting in the wrong order (e.g., descending), forgetting to update timestamps before sorting, and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: def sort_entries(entries): entries.sort(key=lambda x: (x.freq, x.ts)).

examples.trace: Start with array of 3 entries. Sort by frequency first: entry 2 has freq=1, entries 1 and 3 have freq=2, so entry 2 comes first. For entries with same freq (1 and 3), sort by timestamp: entry 3 has ts=8, entry 1 has ts=10, so order is (3,300,2,8) then (1,100,2,10). Final sorted order: (2,200,1,5), (3,300,2,8), (1,100,2,10).

Start with array of 3 entries. Sort by frequency: entry 5 has freq=1, entries 4 and 6 have freq=3, so entry 5 first. For entries with same freq (4 and 6), sort by timestamp: entry 4 has ts=1, entry 6 has ts=3, so order is (4,400,3,1) then (6,600,3,3). Final sorted order: (5,500,1,2), (4,400,3,1), (6,600,3,3).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Evicting Least Frequently Used Item from Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Identify and remove the LFU item from a sorted array of cache entries.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the eviction process in an LFU cache using the sorted array approach. The intuition is that once the array is sorted by frequency and recency, the item to evict is always the first element in the array, as it has the lowest frequency and, if tied, the oldest timestamp. This works because the sorting subroutine ensures the array is in ascending order of frequency and timestamp, so the first entry is the least frequently used (and least recent for ties). The algorithm: given a sorted array of cache entries (each with key, value, frequency, timestamp), if the cache is at capacity and a new item needs to be added, remove the first entry from the array. This is an O(1) operation if using an array where removal from the front requires shifting (O(n) in practice, but conceptually simple). Step-by-step: 1. Check if array length equals capacity. 2. If yes, remove the first element (index 0) from the array. 3. This frees up space for a new entry. The time complexity for eviction itself is O(n) due to shifting in an array, but the key insight is that the decision of which item to evict is made trivial by the sorted order. Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into the key sub-routine by being the action taken after sorting. Common mistakes include evicting the wrong item (e.g., last item), not checking capacity before eviction, and not updating the array properly after removal. Real code pattern: if len(array) == capacity: evicted = array.pop(0).

examples.input: Sorted array: [(2,20,1,5), (1,10,2,10), (3,30,2,8)], capacity=3, need to add new item
examples.trace: Array has 3 entries, at capacity. Sorted order: first entry is (2,20,1,5) with freq=1 (lowest), ts=5. Evict this first entry by removing it from array. Resulting array: [(1,10,2,10), (3,30,2,8)] with 2 entries, now space for new item.

examples.input: Sorted array: [(5,50,1,2), (4,40,3,1), (6,60,3,3)], capacity=3, need to add new item
examples.trace: Array at capacity with 3 entries. Sorted: first entry is (5,50,1,2) with freq=1 (lowest). Evict this entry. New array: [(4,40,3,1), (6,60,3,3)], now has 2 entries.

ladders.title: Building Block — Updating Frequency and Timestamp on Cache Access
ladders.desc: Modify cache entry attributes when accessed via get or put operations.
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the update of frequency and timestamp for a cache entry when it is accessed through a get operation or updated via a put operation. The intuition is that in an LFU cache, frequency tracks how often an item is used, and timestamp tracks when it was last used for tie-breaking. When an item is accessed (e.g., retrieved with get or updated with put), we need to increment its frequency to reflect increased usage and update its timestamp to mark it as more recent. This ensures that the sorting subroutine can correctly order items based on current usage patterns. The algorithm works by: 1. Finding the cache entry for a given key (using linear search in the array approach). 2. Incrementing the frequency field by 1. 3. Updating the timestamp to a new, increasing value (e.g., a global counter). Step-by-step: For a cache entry tuple (key, value, freq, ts), on access: freq = freq + 1, ts = new_timestamp. This update must happen before sorting to reflect the new state. Time complexity is O(1) for the update itself, but finding the entry is O(n) with linear search. Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into the core logic by ensuring that after eviction, the remaining entries have accurate frequencies and timestamps for future sorts. Common mistakes include not incrementing frequency on put updates, using non-monotonic timestamps, and forgetting to update both fields. Real code pattern: for entry in array: if entry.key == key: entry.freq += 1; entry.ts = current_time; break.

examples.trace: Start with entry (1,100,2,10). Increment frequency: 2 + 1 = 3. Update timestamp to new value 15. Result: (1,100,3,15).
Start with entry (5,500,0,5). Increment frequency: 0 + 1 = 1. Update timestamp to 6. Result: (5,500,1,6).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Linear Search in Array for Key
ladders.desc: Find a cache entry by key in an unsorted array using linear search.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves searching for a specific key in an array of cache entries. The intuition is simple: since the array is not indexed by key (it's sorted by frequency and timestamp, not by key), we need to scan through each entry to find the one with a matching key. This works because each entry contains a key, and we can compare it with the target key until a match is found or the end of the array is reached. The algorithm: iterate through each element in the array, check if the key of the current entry equals the target key. If found, return the entry or its index; if not found after checking all elements, return a sentinel value (e.g., -1 or None). Step-by-step: 1. Initialize index = 0. 2. While index < length of array: if array[index].key == target_key: return index. 3. Increment index. 4. If loop ends, return -1. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case, where n is the number of entries, as we may need to check every element. Space complexity is O(1) extra. This feeds into the building block by providing the means to locate an entry for updating frequency and timestamp. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop, not handling empty arrays, and returning the wrong value on miss. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(array)): if array[i].key == key: return i; return -1.

examples.trace: Start with array of 3 entries. Check index 0: key=1, not match. Check index 1: key=2, match. Return index 1.
Array has 2 entries. Check index 0: key=4, not match. Check index 1: key=5, not match. End of array, key 6 not found, return -1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Storing Data in Arrays with Tuples
ladders.desc: Represent cache entries as tuples in an array data structure.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces how to store and manage cache entries using arrays and tuples. The intuition is that in programming, we often need to group related data (like key, value, frequency, timestamp) into a single unit, and arrays allow us to store multiple such units in an ordered list. Tuples are immutable sequences that can hold multiple values, making them ideal for representing a cache entry with fixed attributes. By storing each entry as a tuple in an array, we can easily add, remove, and access entries. The algorithm: define a cache entry as a tuple (key, value, frequency, timestamp). Use an array (list in Python) to hold multiple entries. Basic operations: initialize an empty array, append a new entry, access an entry by index, remove an entry by index. Step-by-step: 1. Create array: entries = []. 2. Add entry: entries.append((key, value, freq, ts)). 3. Access entry at index i: entry = entries[i]; key = entry[0], value = entry[1], etc. 4. Remove entry at index i: entries.pop(i). Time complexity: append is O(1) amortized, access by index is O(1), removal by index is O(n) due to shifting. Space is O(n) for n entries. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the data structure for linear search. Common mistakes include using wrong indices for tuple elements, modifying tuples (they are immutable, so create new ones), and not handling array bounds. Real code pattern: entries = []; entries.append((1, 10, 1, 1)); entry = entries[0]; key = entry[0]; entries.pop(0).

examples.input: Create array, add entry (1,100,1,5), access it, remove it
examples.trace: Start with empty array: []. Add entry: append (1,100,1,5), array becomes [(1,100,1,5)]. Access entry at index 0: (1,100,1,5). Remove entry at index 0 using pop(0), array becomes [].

examples.input: Add entries (2,200,2,10) and (3,300,3,15) to array, access second entry

testCases.input: Add entries (3,30,3,3) and (4,40,4,4) to empty array
